| Liquid Smoke | |

| Category | Extracts and flavorings |

Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients

**Liquid smoke** is a variety of savory flavoring used in foods.

## Production

[edit | edit source]

Two general production methods are described for making liquid smoke. In either case, the process starts with burning damp wood to generate a large amount of smoke.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1) In one method, the smoke is piped through water, whereupon a number of the smoky flavor compounds dissolve into the water.[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2) In another method, the resulting steam and smoke are blown over condensers,[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) which cause the water and flavor compounds to condense into a concentrated liquid.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:6-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6) The liquid may be left to rest, allowing the water-insoluble particles and oils to separate out,[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2) and the water may then be filtered for further refinement.[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6) Some producers will add vinegar, molasses, color, and/or salt.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[7]](#cite_note-:5-7)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Liquid smoke takes the form of a dark, brown liquid.[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2) As expected, the flavor tastes essentially like smoke,[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) with some nuanced notes depending on the variety of wood used to make it (e.g. mesquite, hickory, etc).[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[7]](#cite_note-:5-7)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

When purchasing commercial liquid smoke, look preferentially for those whose ingredients are just smoke and water.[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) Store at room temperature, and avoid shaking it—any unpleasant particulates remaining in the liquid tend to settle over time, and shaking will re-distribute them throughout.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

The principal use of liquid smoke is to add a complex smokey flavor to foods that would be inconvenient or even impossible to properly smoke[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1).[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:6-5)[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) For example, some soups, sauces and marinades, proteins, and beverages may benefit from its use.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) Because of its strength, very little liquid smoke is needed to achieve a desirable flavor,[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:5-7) and too much liquid smoke will result in an unpleasant acrid taste.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) A few drops is a good place to start for most applications.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)
